is a village in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), England.

includes Shotton Colliery village and an area to the west, south and east, but is bordered by the [A19 road](/wiki/A19_road "A19 road") to the east and does not include the village of [Old Shotton](/wiki/Old_Shotton "Old Shotton") which is to the east of that road and forms part of the town of [Peterlee](/wiki/Peterlee "Peterlee").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It has a [parish council](/wiki/Parish_councils_in_England "Parish councils in England").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

**Shotton Colliery** is a village in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), England.

The [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") of **Shotton** includes Shotton Colliery village and an area to the west, south and east, but is bordered by the [A19 road](/wiki/A19_road "A19 road") to the east and does not include the village of [Old Shotton](/wiki/Old_Shotton "Old Shotton") which is to the east of that road and forms part of the town of [Peterlee](/wiki/Peterlee "Peterlee").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It has a [parish council](/wiki/Parish_councils_in_England "Parish councils in England").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shotton_Colliery&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### The village

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shotton_Colliery&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The village")]

Old Shotton can be traced back as far as 900 AD, when it was known as Scitton, which in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") means 'of the Scots', The village name[[4]](#cite_note-4) was first recorded in 1165 as 'Sottun'. By the 16th century, when [Easington](/wiki/Easington,_County_Durham "Easington, County Durham") was under the control of [Prince Bishops](/wiki/Prince_Bishop_of_Durham "Prince Bishop of Durham"), the village had become known as Shotton.

In 1756, the [Brandlings](/wiki/Brandling_of_Newcastle "Brandling of Newcastle") built [Shotton Hall](/wiki/Shotton_Hall "Shotton Hall") and this eventually passed through marriage to the Burdon family.

### The colliery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shotton_Colliery&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The colliery")]

In 1833, the Haswell Coal Company began to sink a [colliery](/wiki/Colliery "Colliery") to the west of Old Shotton, near Shotton Grange Farm. This pit began producing coal the following year, and the village of Shotton Colliery soon started to develop.

The pit was initially prosperous, but it closed on 3 November 3 1877, causing people to leave the village to work at other pits in the area. In 1900, the pit reopened and grew rapidly, leading to an increased population in the village. More housing was built, making other industries, including the Coke Works and the Brick Works, less popular.

By 1947, the original houses, east of the railway line, were in disrepair. Most of the bottom of Front Street was demolished.

In 1972, the [National Coal Board](/wiki/National_Coal_Board "National Coal Board") announced that it was closing the colliery, at a cost of 800 jobs. [Easington District Council](/wiki/Easington_(district) "Easington (district)") built new housing in the 1970s, pulling down most of the remaining pit houses in an attempt to improve the village. Throughout most of the 1970s, work was done to remove the pit heap, which was at one time the largest in the country. The Brick Works and Coke Works went with the pit.

The only pit building left is now used as a second-hand car showroom. The remains of the pit baths remain semi-derelict.

## Present-day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shotton_Colliery&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Present-day")]

Shotton Airfield/Peterlee Parachute Centre now occupies the colliery site, and it is common to see parachutists over the village most days.

Most of the parish's pubs, cinemas and the [railway station](/wiki/Shotton_Bridge_railway_station "Shotton Bridge railway station") have been demolished or converted to other uses. A small number of shops are left.

The village is now almost empty of work. There are a few industrial units close to the airfield and on the opposite side of the village at Thornley Crossings, but the main sources of employment are [call centres](/wiki/Call_center "Call center") which opened east of the village, dividing Shotton Colliery and Old Shotton.

Some of the buildings at Grange Farm remain and now overlook fields as they did in the 1840s, some of which contain the hard runway and hangars and other buildings of the parachute centre.

The war memorial in Front Street is Grade II [listed](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") and is the only listed building in the parish. It has an inscription "Erected 1920 by the parishioners and federated workmen of Shotton Colliery to the memory of the 152 men who fell in the Great War 1914-1919".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Notable residents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shotton_Colliery&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notable residents")]

* [Bill Cockburn](/wiki/Bill_Cockburn "Bill Cockburn") (1937–1995), footballer with [Burnley F.C.](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.") and [Gillingham F.C.](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C."), was born in Shotton Colliery
* [Maurice Cullen](/wiki/Maurice_Cullen_(boxer) "Maurice Cullen (boxer)") (1937–2001), boxer, lived in Shotton Colliery.
* [Rebecca Posner](/wiki/Rebecca_Posner "Rebecca Posner") (1929–2018), Professor of Romance Languages at the University of Oxford, was born in Shotton Colliery

Retrieved 27 May 2024.
